relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The court must distinguish the righteous claim from the guilty one.
Even punishment is placed beneath judicial measure.
The condemned remains a brother whose dignity cannot be beaten away.
The ox may eat while it labors.
A widow without a son is drawn into her deceased husband's kinship line.
The first child is assigned to preserve the dead brother's name.
If the brother refuses, the widow may bring the matter publicly to the gate.
The elders hear his refusal rather than forcing silent compliance.
She removes his sandal and marks the refusal with public shame.
The household is remembered by the duty it declined.
A woman intervenes physically while two men fight.
The ancient law answers her act with irreversible mutilation.
Commerce may not hide two standards in one bag.
The house may not contain one measure for buying and another for selling.
A complete and just measure sustains communal life.
Dishonest trade is named as injustice before YHWH.
Amalek is remembered through an attack during Israel's vulnerability.
The exhausted and straggling were targeted from behind.
The memory becomes a command to erase Amalek, a command no modern people may inherit as a target.

contemplative_note: Deuteronomy 25 reaches for proportion, labor dignity, family continuity, and honest trade, then repeatedly crosses into shame, mutilation, and exterminatory memory. Modern justice must keep the limits and discard the violence.
